crisp_articulations: We are trained to read a price of zero as a verdict that a thing is worth little. The truth is the reverse.
A thing with no price is invisible to a civilization that decides what to protect by reading prices.
The bill was never cancelled. It was sent somewhere you could not see, to someone who could not send it back.
People who do not yet exist make poor plaintiffs.
The whole apparatus of price and law and grievance is built for parties who can show up, and the deepest severed costs are owed to parties who cannot.
A renewing resource taken too fast is a debt you can repay by slowing down. A barrel of oil burned is a debt that cannot be repaid by anyone, ever.
The residual commons value of the barrel is not a discounted flow of future damage. It is the present absence of a thing that will not return... you do not discount the absence of a thing.
Honest accounting lays the cost bare. It does not, by itself, decide who pays it.
Change the world and you do not need a new framework. You only get new numbers.
Honest accounting is not the same as restraint... the guilt was always supposed to attach to the severed cost.
Take freely here; reserve there. That is not a contradiction. It is the framework seeing clearly in both directions at once.
The difference between the two was never that one drilled and the other refrained. Both drilled. The difference is architecture.
Not a politics and not a system, just an honest set of books.

crisp_articulations: An externality names a cost that falls outside a transaction; cost severance names the legal architecture that put it there, and the power asymmetry that keeps the cost-bearer from refusing it.
What happened at Libby was not a moment. It was an architecture.
TARP is the wrong unit of measure.
Cost-severance accounting does not replace this analysis. It prices what the rent-seeking buys.
The architecture knows how to tag cost to value. It simply does so incompletely, and the incompleteness is where the severance lives.
Tractability is not the absence of severance. It is severance proceeding on schedule.
It is the claim that taught the method how to count, and extending the method to asbestos and oil takes nothing from it.
A future generation is, in the most literal sense, dominated by the present one. It cannot contest the decisions being made on its behalf.
What is engineered can be engineered differently.

crisp_articulations: It prices what is permanently removed, and the sun removes nothing.
Marx priced the surplus. Ostrom governed the renewable. Daly warned about the throughput. Hartwick saved the rent. Each of them stood near the empty plot. None of them built on it.
Hartwick saves the proceeds of the sale. The thing that was sold is not coming back.
Daly gives us the warning. He does not give us the invoice.
The discount rate is not a measurement of how much the future is worth. It is a decision about how much the future is worth, carried through the arithmetic as if it were a fact of nature.
It is a number the axis does not have a column for.
Someone creates a cost; someone else absorbs it; and the second party has no way to send the bill back to the first.
The residual commons value is not a slogan. It is a missing line on a balance sheet, and balance sheets do not have politics.
Norway built the escrow. Nigeria did not. The difference was a choice, and the choice was institutional, which means it was available to both.
We have been selling the barrel for the present's hundred dollars, writing zero in the column that should hold the rest, and calling the difference cheap. It was never cheap.

'You buy cheap insurance against a downside bigger than you think.' / 'declining to dig, or declining to sell, which is the cheapest form of storage there is.'

'The same accounting that sees a bill before it comes due can also read one already overdue. Looking forward, it prices what has not yet been taken... Looking backward, it reads a cost already paid by the wrong people.'
'That number is the same kind of object as the gap between four dollars and five hundred: a cost severed from the people who captured the benefit and carried by the people who bore the harm.'
'What the framework offers is the ledger. What belongs on it, looking backward, is theirs to enter.'
'honest pricing is not a departure from the market he described. It is the completion of it. A price that reflects every cost is the price the market was always supposed to produce.'
'None of them ended the problem they described. Each of them changed how the rest of us could see and name and argue about it, which is the precondition for doing anything about it at all.'

crisp_articulations: Whether cost severance is large or small in any given case is not a natural feature of the world. It is a policy choice.
Same physical resource, different architecture, different outcome — the choice is the thing.
An existence proof, in mathematics, does not specify how to construct what it proves possible; it establishes only that the thing can be done.
The financial instrument constructed to capture the rent of the physical resource has, in operational terms, outgrown the resource that created it.
[Hartwick's rule] operates on the consumption stream. It does not reconstitute the stock.
The next generation of Norwegians inherits a wealthier financial economy but a poorer geological one.
Cost severance in the Delta is large because the bond is small, not because Nigerian oil is somehow different in its physical or chemical character from Norwegian oil.
It sought out McDowell because the institutional protection was structurally easier to overwhelm. This is not a malfunction of the extraction economy; it is its operating principle.

[Tooze] has been steadily pointing at this asymmetry without yet naming an instrument that resolves it. The Norwegian case does not resolve it either. It only makes the asymmetry legible as architecture rather than as accident.
Norway's accountability bond is denominated in Norwegian-citizen claims; the climate externality runs to populations with no Norwegian citizenship.

crisp_articulations: A bargain is a price honestly low. This was a price falsely low, and the difference did not vanish.
It was a loan, drawn silently against the future and against the county itself, and the bill has been arriving in installments ever since.
McDowell County did not fail to share in coal's prosperity. McDowell County financed it, at terms no one ever read aloud.
American energy accounting has written a zero where the real costs belong. Not a small number, not a contested number. A zero.
A country that keeps a ledger like that is not thrifty. It is flying blind, mistaking the costs it has hidden for costs it has avoided.

Whoever creates a cost should bear it. That principle does not belong to the left or the right. It is the premise under every functioning market and every theory of personal responsibility ever taught.
The first honest act is not a policy. It is an accounting. (candidate epigraph)
It only looked that way to the people who did not have to pay the rest.
(op-ed.md) a second, unmeasured trade ran in parallel, binding on parties who never sat at the table. (plain-English gloss of the fictitious-commodity move)
